coredata.dc:title: The preparation and mechanism of garlic-templated fluorescent nanoparticles for degradation of Coriolus versicolor via one-pot
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xiaoqi
Wang, Lei
Wang, Yamei
coredata.dc:description: The preparation of wood preservatives from plant extracts has gained popularity in wood protection research because of the advantages of environmental protection. Various plant extracts exert antifungal effects. Nano-modification is an effective method of improving the antifungal properties of plant-derived wood preservatives. Using microwave technology, this study sought to synthesize garlic-templated fluorescent nanoparticles (garlic NPs) exhibiting excellent performance. Garlic NPs are used as wood preservatives, and their fluorescence allows for tracking the distribution and impregnation of garlic NPs in wood. Garlic NPs had nearly 60 times lower minimum inhibitory concentration than garlic extract. In addition, Garlic NPs exerted an effective antifungal effect at a concentration of 0.07 g/mL. The antifungal properties were improved and costs were noticeably reduced with nano-modification. The antifungal preservative inhibited growth by destroying the cell wall and membrane of Coriolus versicolor, which ultimately prevented fungal cell division. This method can be widely used in the nano-modification of other plant extracts.

head.ce:title: The preparation and mechanism of garlic-templated fluorescent nanoparticles for degradation of Coriolus versicolor via one-pot

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The preparation of wood preservatives from plant extracts has gained popularity in wood protection research because of the advantages of environmental protection. Various plant extracts exert antifungal effects. Nano-modification is an effective method of improving the antifungal properties of plant-derived wood preservatives. Using microwave technology, this study sought to synthesize garlic-templated fluorescent nanoparticles (garlic NPs) exhibiting excellent performance. Garlic NPs are used as wood preservatives, and their fluorescence allows for tracking the distribution and impregnation of garlic NPs in wood. Garlic NPs had nearly 60 times lower minimum inhibitory concentration than garlic extract. In addition, Garlic NPs exerted an effective antifungal effect at a concentration of 0.07 g/mL. The antifungal properties were improved and costs were noticeably reduced with nano-modification. The antifungal preservative inhibited growth by destroying the cell wall and membrane of Coriolus versicolor, which ultimately prevented fungal cell division. This method can be widely used in the nano-modification of other plant extracts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data used to support the findings of this study are included within the article and supplementary information file.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent nanoparticles— a new type of nanomaterials—mainly includes light-emitting quantum dots, fluorescent polymer nano/microspheres, and composite fluorescent silica nanoparticles (Farkkila et al., 2021). In the field of quantum dots, carbon, carbon nitride, MXene, and sulfur quantum dots have been developed rapidly because of their unique properties (Ebe et al., 2022; Liyanage et al., 2019). Fluorescent polymer nano/microspheres mainly refer to polystyrene and polyacrylamide particles, and the fluorescent effect is achieved by the surface bonding or adsorption of fluorescein (Qiagedeer et al., 2021). Composite fluorescent silica nanoparticles comprise a functional core and a modified silicon shell. Commonly used preparation methods mainly include reversed-phase microemulsion and improved Stöber hydrolysis (Peerzade et al., 2020). Fluorescent nanoparticles exhibit not only excellent optical properties but also good biocompatibility; they easily achieve surface functionalization and outstanding application potential in the fields of biosensing, antibacterial, and fluorescent probes (Su et al., 2016).

Wood is a natural, renewable, degradable, and recyclable biomass material that has desirable characteristics, such as a beautiful texture, high strength-to-weight ratio, robust processability, and environmental friendliness (Fernandez-Costas et al., 2017; Valette et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2018a; Wang et al., 2018b). However, untreated wood products are prone to biodegradation during the application process, which severely reduces their use-value and service life. The service life and performance of wood are significantly improved after treatment with preservative substances (Bi et al., 2019). Previous studies have found that chemical preservatives can be used to protect the wood; however, heavy metal elements in chemical preservatives are harmful to the environment. Thus, it is imperative to identify and use natural antimicrobial agents as wood preservatives (Qin et al., 2019).

Garlic (Allium sativum L.) is a type of Liliaceae plant that belongs to the Allium family (Chen et al., 2019; Piletti et al., 2019). It is one of the leading varieties of vegetables and spices and is used in food and medicine. The garlic bulb is rich in protein, polysaccharides, and trace elements, and it has bactericidal, anticancer, and other pharmacological functions. The amounts of the garlic sufficient to fulfill the requirements of the growing industry. The main chemical compositions of garlic include allicin, alliin, alliinase, and trace elements (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, selenium, and germanium) (Ansary et al., 2020; Bozin et al., 2008; El-Refai et al., 2018; Gorinstein et al., 2008; Martins et al., 2016; Vlase et al., 2013). Garlic extract exhibits a wide range of antibacterial activities against Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, Klebsiella, Proteus, Bacillus, and Clostridium (Hsieh et al., 2001; Meriga et al., 2012; Ward et al., 2002). Several studies have explored the bactericidal effect of garlic extract on pathogenic bacteria (Belguith et al., 2009; Kim et al., 2007; Lemar et al., 2005; O'Gara et al., 2000); howevr, few studies have focused on the common, wood-rotting fungi.

Recently, nanoparticles containing antifungal and antioxidant substances have been used to prevent and reduce food spoilage and inhibit pathogenic microorganisms (Amalraj et al., 2017; Sandhir et al., 2015). Previous studies have demonstrated the green synthesis of functionalized magnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (FMNPs) (Hoang et al., 2019; Siciliano et al., 2022). Compared with FMNPs, carbon-based materials have the advantages of low cost, stable chemical properties, and environmental sustainability. Garlic extract has high carbon content; hence, it can be used as a green and ecofriendly carbon precursor in the synthesis of fluorescent nanoparticles. Compared with fluorescent nanoparticles synthesized from other carbon sources, those synthesized from natural products have the advantages of low toxicity, high solubility, green synthetic route, good luminescence stability, and biocompatibility (Son et al., 2012; Ye et al., 2013). Although the aqueous extract of garlic, a natural product, has antibacterial properties, the smell of garlic is pungent; moreover, the antibacterial ingredients are not weather-resistant, making them prone to environmental deactivation (Zhao et al., 2021). Therefore, current studies should focus on improving the antifungal and preservative stability properties of garlic extract using appropriate modification methods to widen its application range.

In this work, an ecofriendly wood preservative was prepared through the nano-modification of garlic extract as a carbon source. Garlic NPs were successfully prepared for the first time. Nano-modification enhanced the antifungal properties of garlic extract and removed its pungent odor. Garlic NPs exerted a noticeable antifungal effect by inhibiting the growth of Coriolus versicolor. This study aimed to investigate the structure and optical properties of garlic NPs and their application in wood decay. Furthermore, the antifungal effects of garlic NPs and extract were compared, and the action mechanism of garlic NPs underlying the inhibition of C. versicolor was elucidated. Garlic NPs function as an effective preservative, and this mechanistic study on garlic NPs provides for a reference for the application of plant-derived wood preservatives in the future.

An ultrapure water equipment (resistance value: 16.00 MΩ.cm) was provided by Shanghai Lefeng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Hangzhou, China). Carbamide (CH4N2O), ethanolamine (C2H7NO), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), glucose (C6H12O6), Agar [(C12H18O9)n], sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4·H2O), disodium phosphate (Na2HPO4·H2O), and ethanol (C2H5OH) were supplied by Fortune Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Garlic was obtained from Walmart Inc. (Hohhot, Inner Mongolia, China). All chemical reagents were of analytical grade. C. versicolor (first generation) strain was obtained from the Research Center for Microbial strain Resources (Xian, Shanxi, China). Fresh felled Populus tomentosa (Populus tomentosa Carr.; sapwood) was selected as the wood sample; the size of the wood block was 10 mm × 20 mm× 20 mm (longitudinal × tangential × radial).

The fluorescent properties of garlic NPs were measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FLS9800, Britain). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Perkin-Elmer FT-IR spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer Spectrum GX, United States); the characteristic functional groups of garlic extract and NPs were identified using FT-IR. The results were obtained using a resolution ratio of 4 cm−1 and wave range of 4000–500 cm−1. The surface element composition, relative content, and molecular structure of garlic NPs were determined through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; TPHI-5000versaprobeII, Shanghai, China) and Raman (Thermo Fischer DXR, USA). The excitation source was Alkα X-ray (hv=1486.6 eV); the takeoff angle was 45°. The absorbance of garlic NPs at different wavelengths was measured using an ultraviolet (UV)–visible spectrophotometer (Vis; LAMBDA 650, Shanghai, China). The scanning speed was 200 nm/min, and the scanning wavelength range was 240–800 nm. Before and after treatment, the gross and subcellular structure of C. versicolor cells was observed via biological transmission electron microscopy (HITACHI HT7800, Germany). The stability and particle size of garlic NPs in ultrapure water were measured using the dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique (Nicomp 380, United States). Each sample was measured thrice.

Garlic (75 g) was dissolved in 250 mL of deionized water and boiled for 10 min to prepare an aqueous extract of garlic. Subsequently, various amines (ethanolamine [C2H7NO] and ethylenediamine [C2H8N2]) were selected as nitrogen sources to improve quantum yield. Different garlic treatment solutions were prepared based on various nano-modification methods: a. 1 mL aqueous extract of garlic and 9 mL deionized water were added to a conical flask; b. 1 mL aqueous extract of garlic, 0.20 g ethanolamine (C2H7NO), 1.00 g carbamide (CH4N2O), and 9 mL deionized water were added to a conical flask; c. 1 mL aqueous extract of garlic, 0.09 g ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), 1.00 g carbamide (CH4N2O), and 9 mL deionized water were added to a conical flask; d. solution b was subjected to microwave treatment (power: 500 W; time: 5 min) to obtain a brown powder, which was then dispersed in 10 mL deionized water; e. solution c was subjected to microwave treatment as mentioned above; a brown powder was prepared, which was then dispersed in 10 mL deionized water; f. 10 mL aqueous extract of garlic was subjected to microwave treatment and dissolved in the deionized water of 10 mL; g. 10 mL aqueous extract of garlic; and h. blank: 10 mL deionized water. The preservatives prepared based on different nano-modification treatments were added to the medium prepared for C. versicolor. After the medium was cooled, fungal mycelium was inoculated with the mycelium facing downward. The inoculated medium was incubated at 28 °C (humidity: 75%) for 7 days. The most effective nano-modification treatment was selected based on the diameter of the antifungal inhibition zone.

Garlic extract was used as a precursor to prepare fluorescent nanoparticles via a bottom-up microwave-assisted method. Ethanolamine (C2H7NO) and carbamide (CH4N2O) were added to the aqueous extract of garlic, and the solvent was further carbonized in a microwave reaction system to prepare garlic NPs. Based on the synthesis process, the antifungal properties of garlic extracts were further optimized by controlling the experimental conditions. The 0.03 g/mL concentration of the aqueous extract of garlic was selected for subsequent experiments. For the optimal preparation of garlic NPs, 0.02 g ethanolamine, 1 mL aqueous extract garlic (concentration: 0.03 g/mL), and 1.00 g carbamide were used. The fluorescent nanoparticles were synthesized using the one-pot method in a microwave reaction system (microwave source: 2450 MHz); the reaction continued for 5 min at a microwave power of 500 W. The obtained garlic NPs were ultrasonically dispersed in ultrapure water and centrifuged at 7168 RCF for 5 min to remove the insoluble substances.

For this, 400 g potato was added to 2000 mL distilled water and boiled for 20 min; then, the potato pieces were removed, and the extract was collected in a beaker. Afterward, 45 g dextrose and 30 g agar were added to the extract and this medium (henceforth, potato dextrose agar [PDA]) was sterilized in a high-temperature environment (121 °C) for 40 min. After sterilization, the medium was maintained in a sterile environment.

Subsequently, 10 mL of different concentrations (0.03, 0.06, 0.09, 0.12, 0.15, and 0.18 g/mL) of the aqueous extract of garlic was added to 160 mL PDA medium. Then, different concentrations (10%, 20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, and 100%) of garlic NPs were filter-sterilized and added to 160 mL of PDA medium (0.03 g/mL garlic extract was nano-modified to prepare various concentrations of garlic NPs in the minimum inhibitory concentration [MIC] experiment). Petri plates containing cooled PDA medium were inoculated with C. versicolor and incubated at 28 °C for 7 days. Control medium (without garlic extract) was also incubated for 7 days after inoculation, and the diameters of C. versicolor colonies were recorded from day 3.

The decay resistance of wood samples were tested following the national forestry industry-standard LY/T1283-2011 (Chinese Standard, 2011). Three control groups were selected for the decay resistance test: blank, garlic extract, and garlic NPs. A total of 240 pieces of wood samples with no evident defects were selected. Among them, the 90 untreated wood pieces constituted the blank control group and 150 pieces treated with either garlic extract or garlic NPs constituted the test groups. For the accuracy of the results, the five concentration gradients of the preservative were used and each experiment was repeated five times. Based on the MIC of the garlic extract, the following concentrations were used for the wood decay resistance test: 0.18, 0.22, 0.26, 0.30, and 0.34 g/mL. Similarly, the following concentrations of garlic NPs were used for the wood decay resistance test: 0.03, 0.07, 0.11, 0.15, and 0.19 g/mL.

Specifically, C. versicolor was first inoculated into the PDA medium and cultivated for 7 days, until the fungi covered the medium. The constant weight of the wood after drying at 103 °C was recorded (W
1 ). The wood sample was impregnated under 0.1 MPa vacuum pressure for 40 min to ensure that the wood was soaked well in the agent. The agent on the wood surface was wiped off after impregnation; then, the weight of the wood was recorded (W
2 ). The drug-loaded wood samples were dried, and the weight (W
3 ) was recorded. Simultaneously, the blank samples were placed in a sterilization pot for 40 min. After cooling, the samples were maintained in a sterile environment for subsequent use. The blank and drug-loaded samples were placed onto the PDA plates covered with the fungal mycelia and incubated at 28 °C for 5 weeks. After incubation, the wood samples were taken out and the hyphae on the surface of decayed samples were removed. Finally, the decayed sample was dried and its weight (W
4 ) was recorded. The drug loadings of the wood samples treated with different concentrations of the preservative were obtained using the following equation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobilization of the C. versicolor specimen
ce:section.ce:para: Glutaraldehyde solution was used to immobilize C. versicolor tissues. C. versicolor mycelia were soaked in 2.5% glutaraldehyde solution to observe the changes in cell structure. Specifically, 2.60 g NaH2PO4·H2O and 29.00 g Na2HPO4·H2O were dissolved in 500 mL ultrapure water, and the pH of the solution was adjusted to 7.4. Then, 5 mL of 0.2 M phosphate buffer was added to 5 mL of the diluted 25% glutaraldehyde solution (the ratio of glutaraldehyde and ultrapure water was 1:4). Glutaraldehyde solution (2.5%) was prepared and stored at 4 °C for later use. Subsequently, C. versicolor hyphae treated with garlic extract and NPs were gently scraped with a cell scraper; hyphae samples were also collected from the blank group. Hyphae obtained from the three groups were centrifuged at 1008 RCF for 8 min. Granular cell samples were observed at the bottom of the centrifuge tubes, and fungal tissues were collected in 2 mL centrifuge tubes after centrifugation. The cell samples were slowly added to a precooled (4 °C) glutaraldehyde fixative solution and immersed completely and then stored at 4 °C. The cell samples were dehydrated, polymerized, and trimmed for observation.

The antifungal activity of garlic NPs was dependent on the surface charge and particle size of the nanoparticles; the antibacterial activity could be altered by adding surface amine passivators (Namdari et al., 2017; Wang and Qiu, 2016). The surface functional groups of garlic NPs can be modified to increase toxicity to fungi (Bing et al., 2016). Therefore, various methods of surface passivation were used to evaluate the antifungal properties of garlic extract subjected to various nano-modification treatments. Among the ammonium passivators assessed, ethanolamine (C2H7NO) enhanced the antifungal properties most effectively.

Fig. S1 shows the most efficient nano-modification methods. Compared with the blank group (h), 10% garlic extract exerted no antifungal effect because of its lower concentration. The antifungal effect of the aqueous extract of garlic was assessed using the aforementioned solution g (control group). The toxicity of the aqueous extract of garlic increased with increasing concentration. Compared with solution f (10 mL garlic), the inhibition of C. versicolor growth remained the same before and after the microwave treatment, which indicated that microwave treatment plays no role in nano-modification. Solutions d (ethanolamine-modified garlic) and e (ethylenediamine-modified garlic) represented the results of solutions b (unmodified ethanolamine and garlic) and c (unmodified ethylenediamine and garlic) after microwave treatment, respectively. As shown in Fig. S1, the method used to prepare solution d (ethanolamine-modified garlic) represented the most effective modification method; the evident antifungal effect of this solution indicated successful chemical modification. It can be noted from solutions c (unmodified ethylenediamine and garlic) and e (ethylenediamine-modified garlic) that the antifungal effect of the agent declined after microwave treatment. Active ingredients responsible for antifungal effects might have degraded during the microwave treatment (Yadav et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2017); which indicates that ethylenediamine does not ensure adequate ammonium passivation. The method used to prepare solution d (ethanolamine-modified garlic) represented the most effective nano-modification method, and ethanolamine exhibited relevant antifungal properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design, synthesis, and characterization of garlic NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Garlic extract was used as the reaction precursor and ethanolamine was used as an amine passivator to prepare fluorescent garlic NPs using the one-pot method. The surface passivation treatment was performed during the synthesis of garlic NPs; the solution color was changed from transparent white to solid black, and the surface of particles was coking, indicating that the surface carbonization of garlic extract was successful ( Fig. 1). The surface morphology of the synthesized garlic NPs was analyzed via transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The results confirmed that garlic NPs had been synthesized successfully (Fig. 1 a-c). The morphology of the prepared garlic NPs was circular, and the TEM observation indicated that the particle size of the prepared garlic NPs was approximately 140 nm. The prepared garlic NPs were ultrasmall carbon nanoparticles with good dispersibility and no observable overlapping adhesion (Fig. 1 a). The garlic NPs exhibited a uniform, circular particle size and the presence of carbon nuclei (Fig. 1 b, c). TEM results showed that garlic extract was successfully deposited into fluorescent nanoparticles, and the DLS results confirmed that the size of the prepared garlic NPs was 140 nm (Fig. 1 d). The particle size of garlic NPs is its essential characteristic feature in wood preservation. The estimated particle size of 140 nm suggested that these particles can enter and attach to the surface of wood cell wall and thus protect it from C. versicolor (Xu et al., 2021).

After UV irradiation, garlic NPs exhibited excellent blue-fluorescent properties (Fig. 1 e). In brief, 2.5 mL of garlic NPs was added to a quartz cuvette and the optimal fluorescence intensity was determined by changing the wavelength. The excitation–emission spectra of garlic NPs are shown in Fig. 1 f. With increasing wavelength, the excitation and emission spectra of garlic NPs first increased and then decreased. The excitation spectrum overlapped with the emission spectrum at the wavelength of 390 nm, indicating the reabsorption phenomenon (Lin et al., 2018; Sinibaldi et al., 2018). The best fluorescence emission peak of the garlic NPs was noted at the optimal excitation wavelength of 435 nm. The optimum emission wavelength was 516 nm. The chemical and optical properties of garlic NPs were directly related to functional groups and surface defects. The garlic NPs exhibited satisfactory fluorescent properties. The excellent fluorescent properties of garlic NPs allowed for the tracking of the impregnation and distribution of garlic NPs after wood treatment.

The surface element composition, chemical valence, and molecular structure of garlic NPs were analyzed using XPS. Three distinct peaks were noted at 530, 398, and 288 eV in the XPS full spectrum analysis of garlic NPs, which corresponded to O1s, N1s, C1s ( Fig. 2 a). XPS images showed that garlic NPs had three elements of C, N, and O; the element contents were 47.33%, 27.63%, and 24.96%, respectively. The XPS spectra of high-resolution C1s of garlic NPs exhibited four deconvoluted peaks at 282.875, 283.75, 284.625 eV, and 287.5 eV, which corresponded to CC, C-N, C-C, and O-CO functional groups, respectively (Fig. 2 b). One peak after deconvolution of the XPS spectrum of O1s was noted at 530 eV, which corresponded to the CO/C-O group (Fig. 2 c). Fig. 2 d represented the XPS spectra of N1s and was deconvolved into peaks at 398.875 and 398 eV, which corresponded to C-N/CN, -N-, and -NH (Lee et al., 2018).

The functional groups of garlic extract and NPs were characterized by recording the vibrations of various functions. The FT-IR spectrum is shown in Fig. 3 a. The absorption peak at 3300 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration of the intramolecular aromatic C-H bond, the absorption peak at 3347 cm−1 represented the stretching vibration peak of the -OH group, and the presence of the -OH group enhanced the hydrophilicity and stability of garlic NPs. This was consistent with the XPS results. The characteristic peaks of the garlic NPs obtained at 2818, 1054, and 763 cm−1 were due to the stretching vibration of C-O, C-O-C, and benzene ring substitution regions, respectively. These results indicated that the garlic NPs primarily comprised aromatic skeletons. The characteristic peak between 1475 cm−1 and 1000 cm−1 was the fingerprint region of the spectrum, which reflected the subtle changes in the molecular structure; the structural changes were mainly due to the in-plane bending vibrations and other equivalent tensile vibrations of C-H, C-O, and C-X (halogens). The characteristic peaks of garlic at 3300 cm−1 and 1653 cm−1 were due to the stretching vibrations of C-H and SO, respectively. The natural skeleton of the garlic carbon structure was retained after surface carbonization. The C-H and SO bonds were preserved, and new chemical bonds were synthesized during the surface modification of garlic, which indicated that the internal structure of garlic was changed during the synthesis (Ma et al., 2018).

The performance and associated cost of garlic NPs, other plant-derived extracts, and chemical preservatives currently used in the market were analyzed; the results are shown in Fig. 3 b. The cost of garlic NPs was significantly lower than that of other chemical and plant-based wood preservatives exerting the same antifungal effect. Garlic NPs were assessed for UV–Vis absorption; the absorption spectrum is shown in Fig. 3 c. The nanoparticles exhibited broad absorption in the UV range and exhibited characteristic peaks at 286.4 and 327.8 nm. It was attributed to the n-π * transition of CO, n-p * transition of carbonyl, and typical absorption of the aromatic π system (Liang et al., 2013). The Raman spectrum showed a broad peak at 1517 cm−1 (Fig. 3 d), characterized by the G band of the garlic NPs. The G-peak showed the sp2 vibration of the graphitized structure of garlic NPs; the Raman test result of garlic NPs was consistent with FT-IR and XPS results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifungal effects of garlic extract and NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The antifungal effects of garlic extract and NPs were evaluated to determine the respective MICs on a PDA medium. Garlic extract and NPs inhibited the growth of C. versicolor; the effect of garlic NPs was more evident than that of garlic extract ( Fig. 4). Fig. 4 a shows the fungal colony on the blank medium, indicating an adequate growth of C. versicolor. The competent growth state of fungus suggested acceptable activity of C. versicolor. Fig. 4 b shows the colony images obtained after garlic extract and NP treatments. The antifungal properties of nano-modified garlic extract were improved. The antifungal effects of different concentrations of garlic extract are shown in Fig. 4 c. Garlic NPs were prepared by diluting the garlic extract to 0.03 g/mL; the antifungal effect of garlic NPs was significantly enhanced after nano-modification (Fig. 4 d). The MICs was determined using different concentrations of garlic extract and NPs. The MIC of garlic extract was 0.18 g/mL (Fig. 4 e) and that of garlic NPs was 100% (the concentration of 100% was prepared by the nano-modification of 0.03 g/mL garlic extract; other concentrations of garlic extract were prepared through dilution; Fig. 4 f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Decay resistance of wood treated with garlic extract and NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The evaluation method of wood decay resistance was based on the rate of wood weight loss after fungal decay. The weight loss rate of drug-loaded wood was <10%; thus, strong decay resistance was noted as per the Chinese Standard GB/T 13942-2009 (Chinese Standard, 2009). Based on the MICs of garlc extract and NPs, different concentrations of bactericidal liquid were used for wood preservation. Wood treated with the preservative under vacuum pressure showed suitable results (Table S1). The influence of concentration on the drug loading of wood generally showed a positive trend.

The antifungal effect of the wood indoor decay resistance test was consistent with the results of previous MIC inhibition zone test. The results of 5-week decay resistance are shown in Fig. 5 a-b. Compared with the blank group, garlic extract and NPs exerted a specific inhibitory effect on fungal growth, but the resistance potential of garlic NPs was more evident than that of garlic extract. Fig. 5 a shows that the weight loss rate of the blank wood samples was 35 (±0.5)%; there was no significant difference in the antifungal effects of the garlic extract (0.18 g/mL) and blank groups. With increasing concentration, the antifungal effect also increased; however, the effect was not prominent. Although the concentration of garlic extract was increased to 0.34 g/mL, the weight loss rate was 22(±0.5)%, indicating it conferred only slight resistance to fungal decay, which was consistent with MIC data that garlic extract exerted a poor antifungal effect. Fig. 5 a shows that the weight loss rate of wood treated with garlic NPs was minimal. When the concentration of garlic NPs was 0.03 g/mL, the weight loss rate of the sample was 14 (±0.5)%, which is close to the strong decay resistance grade. When the concentration was 0.07 g/mL, the rate was 9.5 (±0.5)%, which met the cutoff for strong decay resistance. The weight loss rate decreased with increasing concentration, and at 0.19 g/ mL, the rate was 4.0(±0.5)%. However, a high concentration is not required for industrial production as the concentration of 0.07 g/mL meets the standard. The concentration of garlic NPs was nearly 60 times lower than that of garlic extract exhibiting the same antifungal effect. This result is highly interesting. To ensure reliability, the weight loss rate was represented by the average of multiple replicates. The comparison of garlic extract and NPs showed that the nano-modification of garlic extract was necessary, and using garlic NPs as the wood preservative was beneficial. As shown in Fig. 5 b, the mycelia of the three groups of control samples exhibited a good growth state, which indicates that the activity of the selected strains was sufficient. The surface of the blank samples was completely covered by fungal mycelia, and the distribution of mycelia on the surface of the drug-loaded wood samples also varied. The mycelia on the surface of wood samples began to decrease gradually with increasing preservative concentration, which was more evident in the wood samples treated with garlic NPs than in those treated with garlic extract; this indicates that garlic NPs exert a remarkable inhibitory effect on fungal growth (Wang et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism underlying the degradation of C. versicolor by garlic NPs
ce:section.ce:para: Garlic extract exerted a poor inhibitory effect on C. versicolor, but garlic NPs exerted a better antifungal effect than garlic extract. Garlic NPs with high nitrogen content affected mycoproteins that were closely related to fungal development (Sakamoto, 2018; Xing et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2019). The MIC of garlic NPs showing effective antifungal effects on C. versicolor led to the destruction of fungal cells and reduction of wood decay with increasing preservative concentration (Huang et al., 2019; Lv et al., 2020; Rojas-Andrade et al., 2017; Vasconcelos et al., 2018). The cell membrane plays a key role in maintaining the stability of the intracellular environment, material transport, energy, and information transfer. Mitochondria represent a crucial cell organelle responsible for energy production, pH stabilization, redox reactions, membrane potential transfer, and apoptosis regulation (Kong et al., 2020; Wolkers et al., 2019; Dumont et al., 2021; Zeng et al., 2020). Garlic NPs disrupted the integrity of the fungal cell membrane, which significantly affected C. versicolor growth. This proves the usefulness of garlic NPs as an antifungal agent. TEM analysis was performed to elucidate the mechanism underlying the antifungal effects of garlic NPs on C. versicolor (Can et al., 2019, 2018; Zheng et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2017). The cell membrane and wall of the blank group samples were intact without rupture; the cells had a clear and complete oval structure, and the mitochondria in the cells were visible. TEM results showed that the fungal cells had a vacuum ring structure ( Fig. 6 a-d). The highlighted red circle in Fig. 6 e-h indicated that the garlic extract entered the interior of C. versicolor. Garlic extract ruptured the cell wall and membrane of C. versicolor, and the cell contents leaked, resulting in the death of the fungal cell. Fig. 6 g shows that garlic extract prevented further division during the cell division stage of C. versicolor, which was the primary action mechanism underlying fungal nongrowth. In contrast, the yellow circles highlighted in Fig. 6 i-l indicate that the garlic NPs pierced the cell membrane and wall of the C. versicolor; the mitochondria were damaged, and finally, the mitochondrial membrane was ruptured. Fig. 6 i-l shows that a large amount of cell contents were leaked, suggesting the excellent antifungal activity of garlic NPs. The groups on garlic NPs were positively charged and C. versicolor was negatively charged; thus, an electrostatic interaction occurred between the fungus and garlic NPs, resulting in the release (pumping-out) of a high number of hydrogen ions from the mitochondrial matrix. Mitochondrial membrane potential was elevated abnormally; the abnormal increase in the membrane potential led to osmotic swelling of the mitochondria. Then, the outer mitochondrial membrane ruptured and the cell contents leaked, resulting in cell death. The abovementioned experimental result indicates that the antifungal activity of garlic has an important causal relationship with the abnormal functions of the mitochondrial and cell membranes (Cushnie et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2017; Xi et al., 2016).

The permeability and ultrastructure of fungal cell membrane treated with garlic NPs was observed using TEM; the antifungal action mechanism of garlic NPs involved cell adhesion and rupture. Garlic NPs exerted the antifungal effect primarily by destroying the cell wall and membrane of C. versicolor. Garlic NPs embedded into the cell membrane to form channels, the internal and external environments of the cell membrane was disturbed, the cell contents were leaked, and the membrane potential was lost. The permeability of the C. versicolor cell membrane was altered, resulting in the disorder in the exchange of substances via the cell membrane. The osmotic pressure inside and outside of the cell membrane changed, and eventually, the cell wall ruptured and disintegrated. Subsequently, garlic NPs acted on mitochondria; the activity of mitochondrial dehydrogenase activity was inhibited in this process, and the number of enzymes synthesized in the cell was reduced, resulting in an inability to meet the requirements for normal cell metabolism. The mycelium of C. versicolor grew abnormally; it disintegrated and then died. Thus, the garlic NPs exerted an inhibitory effect on C. versicolor (Pan et al., 2017, 2019; Valdez-Salas et al., 2021).

In this study, ecofriendly garlic NPs were prepared successfully. The nano-modification method improved the antifungal properties of garlic extract and removed its pungent odor; this indicates that the nano-modification was successful. In future studies, the concentration of garlic NPs should be increased to further explore the inhibition of C. versicolor. Garlic NPs exhibit excellent fluorescence and antifungal activities, which can be utilized in the field of drug delivery. Garlic NPs are expected to resolve issues associated with the observability and traceability that the traditional drug carriers do not ensure. Garlic NPs have potential as antimicrobial agents and may facilitate the development of novel drug carriers.

The antifungal properties of garlic extract and NPs were evaluated using PDA medium inoculated with C. versicolor. The MIC results showed that C. versicolor was sensitive to both garlic extract and NPs. The MIC of garlic extract was 0.18 g/mL and that of garlic NPs was 0.03 g/mL. The effective concentration was reduced by approximately 60 times in the case of garlic NPs, which indicated successful nano-modification of the garlic extract. The preservation of wood treated with garlic NPs was evidently improved; the wood weight loss rate was reduced from 35 (±0.5)% to 4.0 (±0.5)%, suggesting successful modification. The surface groups of garlic NPs improved leaching resistance in wood. The fluorescent properties of garlic NPs helped observe the distribution of the preservatives in the wood. The garlic NPs inhibited fungal growth by destroying the cell wall, cell membrane, and mitochondria, thus effectively inhibiting fungal cell division. Therefore, garlic NPs should be considered as a candidate for the development of safe and sustainable wood preservatives.

Xiaoqi Zhao: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Lei Wang: Formal analysis, Validation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Xiaoqi Zhao and Lei Wang contributed to this work equally. Yamei Wang: Conceptualization, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.
